summary: Asparouhova examines the paradox that 40% of millennials support government censorship of speech offensive to minorities, despite being the most tolerant generation on social issues. She argues this isn't really about government policy but about a new form of peer-enforced censorship enabled by social media, where outrage content spreads fastest because it reinforces identity. Drawing on Scott Alexander's 'toxoplasma of rage' concept, she suggests that online mob dynamics and public shaming function as de facto censorship even without government action. She concludes that while the impulse comes from good intentions, society must decide whether it still values free speech as a civil right.
key_points: 40% of millennials support government censorship of speech offensive to minorities, contradicting both First Amendment principles and their own reputation as the most tolerant generation
Increased access to information has paradoxically made people more inclined toward censorship rather than less, which is counterintuitive
Online outrage culture functions as a form of distributed censorship through public shaming and mob dynamics, regardless of government involvement
The spread of extreme perspectives online is driven by identity reinforcement rather than genuine ideological shifts in the population
The protection of free speech matters most in edge cases involving speech people find offensive, not speech everyone agrees with
money_quotes: It seems counterintuitive that increased access to information online should make us want to censor more, not less.
How does one mesh that utopian worldview with the desire to censor others who are different?
These are the edge cases where the protection of free speech matters most.
The content that pisses us off the most is also the content we share the most, because it strengthens our identity.
Someone wrote an offensive tweet or made an off-color remark? May a thousand fires rain upon them! These days, with enough angry retweets, anyone can censor anything.
Writing an angry post about so-and-so's latest offensive remark is technically an exercise of your right to free speech, but as part of a critical mass, also contributes to censorship of your peers.

World peace is here, but it's a little dystopian. Rather than the colorful patchwork quilt envisioned by 1960s free-love hippies, we've got citizens policing adherence to a politically correct set of views.
key_insight: Modern censorship is no longer a government undertaking but a distributed social phenomenon where online outrage mobs enforce conformity through public shaming, making each individual's angry retweet a small act of collective censorship.
